perturbation.original_text: 8.3 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the law of England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Media Releases No media releases, public announcements or public disclosure by either party relating to this Agreement, its subject matter or the Information, including but not limited to promotional or marketing, shall be made without the prior written consent of the other party. 8. General 8.1 Notices Any notices required under this Agreement shall be in wr iting and posted by registered mail return receipt requested to the applicable address indicated in Exhibit A. 8.2 Entire Agreement This Agreement and any attachments hereto: i) are the complete and exclusive statement between the parties with respect to the protection of the confidentiality of the Information; and ii) supersede all related discussions and other communications between the parties; and iii) may only be modified in writing by authorised representatives of the parties. 8.3 Governing Law No media releases, public announcements or public disclosure by either party relating to this Agreement, its subject matter or the Information, including but not limited to promotional or marketing, shall be made without the prior written consent of the other party. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the law of England and shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause has been moved to the Media Release section. This creates confusion as to whether the agreement is bound by its governing law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English law regarding contract interpretation requires governing law clauses to be clearly and unambiguously presented.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the governing law clause to an unrelated section undermines its prominence, potentially violating the principle of clear and unambiguous contract terms under English law. The new location implies that media releases dictates the governing law of the agreement. This makes it more difficult to enforce the agreement under English law, as its applicability could be questioned.

You are here: BAILII >> Databases >> England and Wales Court of Appeal (Civil Division) Decisions >> O'Brien v Department for Constitutional Affairs [2008] EWCA Civ 1448 (19 December 2008) URL: https://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWCA/Civ/2008/1448.html Cite as: [2009] IRLR 294, [2008] EWCA Civ 1448, [2009] 2 CMLR 15, [2009] ICR 593 [ New search ] [ Context ] [ View without highlighting ] [ Printable RTF version ] [Buy ICLR report: [2009] ICR 593 ] [ Help ] Neutral Citation Number: [2008] EWCA Civ 1448 Case No: A2/2008/1123 IN THE SUPREME COURT OF JUDICATURE COURT OF APPEAL (CIVIL DIVISION) ON APPEAL FROM THE EMPLOYMENT APPEAL TRIBUNAL UKEAT 013907ZT Royal Courts of Justice Strand, London, WC2A 2LL 19/12/2008 B e f o r e : CHANCELLOR OF THE HIGH COURT LADY JUSTICE SMITH and LORD JUSTICE MAURICE KAY ____________________ Between: O'Brien Appellant - and - Department for Constitutional Affairs Respondent ____________________ (Transcript of the Handed Down Judgment of WordWave International Limited A Merrill Communications Company 190 Fleet Street, London EC4A 2AG Tel No: 020 7404 1400, Fax No: 020 7831 8838 Official Shorthand Writers to the Court) ____________________ Mr Robin Allen QC and Miss Rachel Crasnow (instructed by Messrs Browne Jacobson) for the Appellant Mr John Cavanagh QC (instructed by Treasury Solicitors) for the Respondent Hearing dates : 12, 13 November 2008 ____________________ HTML VERSION OF JUDGMENT ____________________ Crown Copyright © Lord Justice Maurice Kay : Recorders play an important part as part-time judges in the Crown Court and the County Courts. Most are in full-time practice as barristers or solicitors, although some have left practice and hold full-time positions as, for example, District Judges. The focus of this case is upon those who remain in practice but sit on a part-time basis. They are remunerated on a fee-paid daily basis. Whether or not they continue in practice beyond the age of 65, they are stood down as Recorders at that age. The main reason for this is to create vacancies for younger Recorders from whom, it is expected, most of the future full-time judiciary will be appointed. Until recently, no-one would have thought that a Recorder qualified for a pro rata judicial pension on retirement. The issue raised on this appeal is whether the Part-time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000 (the PTWR), which is founded upon and purports to implement the Part-time Workers Framework Directive 97/81/EC) (the Directive), either entitles or disentitles Recorders to or from the protection of part-time workers against discrimination. The appellant was a Recorder from 1978 until his retirement at the age of 65 on 31 March 2005. He is one of a number of part-time judicial office holders who have instituted proceedings in the Employment Tribunal claiming that protection. The claim may appear to be a bold one since Regulation 17 of the PTWR provides: "These Regulations do not apply to any individual in his capacity as a holder of a judicial office if he is remunerated on a daily fee-paid basis." It is common ground that that provision is not susceptible to an interpretation favourable to the appellant. However, his case is that Regulation 17 must be disapplied because of the direct effect of
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the governing law clause creates a structural flaw by placing it within the media release section, potentially causing confusion about its applicability to the entire agreement. The Law Explanation states this 'undermines its prominence' and could make it 'more difficult to enforce the agreement under English law'. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction', where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult to interpret.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Injunctive Rights Each party acknowledges and agrees that the Information is the confidential and/or propri etary and/or trade secret information of the other and the unauthorised use or disc losure of the Information could cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the other party for which that other party would have no adequate remedy at law. Therefore, each party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have at law or in equity, to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief in respect of any breach or potential breach of this Agreement by the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Business Purpose The parties expressly agree that the provision of Inform ation hereunder and any discussions held in connection with the Business Purpose shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties provided that no breach of this Agreement is so occasion ed or oblige that party to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Business Purpose. Any forecasts or similar material provided by either party to the other shall not constitute any commitments. 6. Injunctive Rights The parties expressly agree that the provision of Inform ation hereunder and any discussions held in connection with the Business Purpose shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties provided that no breach of this Agreement is so occasion ed or oblige that party to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Business Purpose. Any forecasts or similar material provided by either party to the other shall not constitute any commitments. Each party acknowledges and agrees that the Information is the confidential and/or propri etary and/or trade secret information of the other and the unauthorised use or disc losure of the Information could cause irreparable harm and significant injury to the other party for which that other party would have no adequate remedy at law. Therefore, each party shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have at law or in equity, to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief in respect of any breach or potential breach of this Agreement by the other.
perturbation.explanation: The text from the Business Purpose section is inserted in between the Injunctive Rights section. This creates a contradiction because Business Purpose discussion is duplicated inside Injunctive Rights, implying that a party may pursue similar discussions with third parties even if it violates the confidential and/or propriety agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under English contract law, a contract should clearly define the circumstances under which a party can seek injunctive relief, especially when confidential information is at stake. Contradictory language undermines this clarity.
perturbation.law_explanation: By adding contradictory language to the injunctive relief clause, the modified text creates uncertainty about the circumstances under which injunctive relief can be sought. Inserting the business purpose text between the section implies that engaging in similar discussions with third parties is allowed even if confidential information is at stake. This ambiguity could weaken a party's ability to obtain an injunction, contradicting the purpose of the clause and making it harder to enforce.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that the Business Purpose discussion is duplicated inside the Injunctive Rights section, implying that a party may pursue similar discussions with third parties even if it violates the confidential agreement. The law explanation indicates this creates uncertainty and weakens a party's ability to obtain an injunction, which contradicts the purpose of the clause. The injected text allows discussions with third parties even if confidential information is at stake, directly undermining the injunctive rights designed to protect confidential information. The scraped snippets do not directly relate to the contradiction as they refer to the Senior Courts Act 1981, however, the structural flaw introduces contradictory conditions within the Injunctive Rights section, making it unclear when those rights apply, and introducing a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Business Purpose The parties expressly agree that the provision of Inform ation hereunder and any discussions held in connection with the Business Purpose shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties provided that no breach of this Agreement is so occasion ed or oblige that party to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Business Purpose. Any forecasts or similar material provided by either party to the other shall not constitute any commitments.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Business Purpose The parties expressly agree that the provision of Inform ation hereunder and any discussions held in connection with the Business Purpose shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties provided that no breach of this Agreement is so occasion ed or oblige that party to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Business Purpose. Any forecasts or similar material provided by either party to the other shall not constitute any commitments. 4. No Further Rights The parties expressly agree that the provision of Inform ation hereunder and any discussions held in connection with the Business Purpose shall not prevent either party from pursuing similar discussions with third parties provided that no breach of this Agreement is so occasion ed or oblige that party to take, continue or forego any action relating to the Business Purpose. Any forecasts or similar material provided by either party to the other shall not constitute any commitments. perturbation.explanation: The text from the 'Business Purpose' section has been inserted into the 'No Further Rights' section. This creates a contradiction because Business Purpose discussion is duplicated inside No Further Rights, implying that pursuing similar discussions with third parties is allowed even if it violates that no further rights are allowed in the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under English Law, contract terms relating to intellectual property rights should be clearly delineated and not be undermined by unrelated contractual clauses.
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988, Section 173
perturbation.law_explanation: By inserting the 'Business Purpose' clause into the 'No Further Rights' section, the altered text creates contradiction about the scope of intellectual property protection under the NDA. This implies that a party may pursue similar discussions with third parties and it doesn't violate no further rights. This weakens the restrictions on using the confidential information, which contradicts the act.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that the 'Business Purpose' clause, allowing discussions with third parties, has been inserted into the 'No Further Rights' section. This contradicts the purpose of the 'No Further Rights' clause, which is to restrict the use of confidential information. The LLM's Law Explanation states that this change weakens restrictions on using confidential information and thus contradicts the intention of protecting intellectual property. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly mention NDAs or intellectual property rights, it refers to the 'Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988', implying that the purpose of the original clause was to protect these rights, which is undermined by the change. This qualifies as a Structural Flaw that creates a Legal Contradiction.
